In the court before the tabernacle, where people gathered, was an altar, to which they must bring their sacrifices, and on which priests must offer them to God. It was made of wood overlaid with brass. A grate of brass was set into the hollow of the altar, about the middle of which the fire was kept, and the sacrifice burnt. It was made like a net or sieve, so that ashes could fall through. This brazen altar was a symbol of Christ dying to make atonement for our sins. The wood would have been consumed by the fire from heaven if it had not been secured by the brass; nor could the human nature of Christ have borne the wrath of God if it had not been supported by Divine power.

The tabernacle was enclosed in a court, about sixty yards long and thirty yards wide, formed by curtains hung upon brazen pillars, fixed in brazen sockets. Within this enclosure priests and Levites offered sacrifices, and Jewish people were admitted. These distinctions represented the difference between the visible nominal church, and the true spiritual church, which alone has access to God, and communion with him.

The pure oil signified the gifts and graces of the Spirit, which all believers receive from Christ, the good Olive, and without which our light cannot shine before men. Priests were to light the lamps and tend them. It is the work of ministers, by preaching and explaining the Scriptures, which are like a lamp, to enlighten the church, God’s tabernacle upon earth. Blessed be God, this light is not now confined to the Jewish tabernacle, but is a light to lighten the gentiles, and for salvation unto the ends of the earth.
